proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that superior, targeted suppression of 4E-BP1 phosphorylation will uniquely rewire the cancer cell translatome, preferentially inhibiting the translation of a network of pro-growth and pro-survival mRNAs, leading to more potent and durable suppression of tumor growth compared to conventional mTORC1 inhibition.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-omics approach. First, we will use a structure-guided kinase interactome analysis (building on Cantley's atlas work) to identify novel, specific inhibitors or protein-protein interaction disruptors targeting the mTORC1-4E-BP1 interface. In PTEN-deficient and other cancer models, we will apply phosphoproteomics and the KSEA (Kinase-Substrate Enrichment Analysis) algorithm to quantify kinase pathway inhibition specificity. Concurrently, we will perform ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq) and RNA-seq to define changes in translation efficiency (TE) for specific mRNAs. Tumor growth will be assessed in orthotopic and PDX models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, mechanistic link between the degree of 4E-BP1 hypophosphorylation and a defined 'vulnerable translatome' in cancer. It will validate 4E-BP1 as a superior therapeutic node over broad mTORC1 inhibition and potentially identify novel, translationally regulated biomarkers of response. The findings could redirect therapeutic development towards specific 4E-BP1-targeting agents.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond observing 4E-BP1 phosphorylation status to functionally decoding the translational consequences of its superior inhibition, integrating cutting-edge phosphoproteomic kinase inference with ribosome profiling to map a pharmacologically induced translational network in tumors.

proposals.title: Co-evolutionary Networks as Predictive Maps of Allosteric Escape from Targeted Kinase Inhibitor Combinations
proposals.motivation: Tumors inevitably develop resistance to targeted kinase inhibitors, often through allosteric mutations that rewire signaling networks, a process poorly predicted by current models. This therapeutic failure represents a major clinical challenge in oncology. A predictive framework for the most likely resistance mutations, particularly those emerging under multi-node inhibition, would allow for the proactive design of more durable treatment strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Co-evolving residue networks in oncogenic kinases and their regulatory proteins (e.g., PTEN, transcription factors) define latent allosteric hotspots; selective pressure from combination kinase inhibitors will preferentially drive mutations within these predicted networks, enabling accurate forecasting of clinical resistance mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will apply Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) to large multiple sequence alignments of human kinome members and key interactors (e.g., from the Atlas of kinase-transcription factor docking) to infer co-evolving residue networks. These networks will be mapped onto 3D structures to identify allosteric hotspots. Using in silico models of selective pressure from inhibitor combinations (e.g., targeting PI3K and MAPK pathways), we will predict which network perturbations are most likely to confer fitness. Predictions will be validated by introducing candidate mutations into isogenic cell lines and patient-derived models, treating with the specified combinations, and assessing phosphoproteomic signaling rewiring and viability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational tool that maps co-evolutionary signatures onto resistance prediction for combination therapies. It will directly link fundamental evolutionary constraints on protein structure to clinically observed escape mechanisms, moving the field from descriptive cataloguing of resistance mutations to proactive forecasting. The validated predictions will provide a rationale for designing next-generation inhibitors or drug sequences that preemptively block the forecasted escape routes.
proposals.novelty: While DCA has been used for structural prediction, its application to forecast the evolutionary trajectory of tumors under specific, multi-node pharmacological pressure is novel. This integrates evolutionary biophysics with cancer therapeutics to predict resistance before it manifests in patients.

proposals.title: Combinatorial Kinase Inhibitor Landscapes Reveal Context-Specific Genetic Dependencies via CRISPR Screening in Polypharmacological States
proposals.motivation: Cancer cells exhibit profound metabolic and signaling plasticity, allowing them to evade single-agent targeted therapies. While combination therapies are promising, identifying robust, context-specific synthetic lethal interactions—particularly those only revealed under multi-pathway inhibition—remains a major challenge. Drawing an analogy from microbial ecology, where combinatorial stressor screens reveal unique vulnerabilities, offers a novel framework to systematically map genetic dependencies in cancer cells under polypharmacological pressure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that preconditioning cancer cells with specific combinations of kinase inhibitors will create unique 'pharmacological states' that unveil a distinct set of genetic dependencies, which are not observable under single-agent or basal conditions, thereby revealing potent and selective targets for combination therapy.
proposals.method: We will employ a genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screen in isogenic PTEN-wildtype and -null cancer cell lines, preconditioned with a fixed, sub-lethal combination of inhibitors targeting key nodes in the PI3K/AKT and MAPK pathways. Screen hits will be validated using high-throughput combinatorial drug-genetic interaction assays. Kinase activity and signaling flux in the polypharmacological state will be profiled using phosphoproteomics, with data analyzed via the kinase-substrate enrichment analysis (KSEA) framework and integrated with our prior kinase-transcription factor interactome atlas to infer mechanistic networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a first-of-its-kind map of genetic dependencies exclusive to multi-kinase inhibited states, directly identifying candidate synthetic lethal partners for established targeted therapy combinations. It will provide a generalizable screening paradigm, borrowed from microbial ecology, for discovering context-specific therapeutic vulnerabilities in cancer and other complex diseases.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of a combinatorial stressor screening concept from microbial ecology to mammalian signaling networks and in performing functional genomics under a fixed, multi-inhibitor precondition—a departure from standard screens that test genetic and pharmacological perturbations additively.

proposals.title: Mapping Synthetic Lethal Vulnerabilities to Competitive Docking Perturbations in Oncogenic Kinase Signaling Networks
proposals.motivation: Kinase signaling networks exhibit robustness through multiplexed, often cooperative, interactions between kinases and their substrates or transcription factors, a principle highlighted by recent kinase-transcription factor interactome atlases. In cancer, this buffering capacity allows tumors to resist simple kinase inhibition. The functional consequences of forcing these networks into a competitive, rather than cooperative, docking regime—potentially mimicking a pathological state—remain unexplored as a source of therapeutic vulnerability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that enforcing competition for specific kinase docking surfaces, such as those used by transcription factors, will create unique genetic dependencies (synthetic lethalities) that are not revealed by standard kinase inhibition screens.
proposals.method: We will engineer isogenic cell lines (e.g., PTEN-wildtype and -null) to express high-affinity, inert decoy peptides that saturate defined kinase docking motifs (e.g., D-motifs, FXFP). Using these engineered lines as backgrounds, we will perform genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screens to identify genes whose loss is selectively lethal under competitive docking perturbation. Hits will be validated and mechanistically characterized using phosphoproteomics and kinase activity inference algorithms (like KSEA or similar) to map the rewired signaling state.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind map of synthetic lethal interactions specific to competitive signaling node occupancy. It will identify a new class of potential drug targets for cancers driven by kinases like PI3K/AKT, where PTEN loss or oncogenic mutations may alter docking equilibria, offering strategies to overcome signaling network robustness.
proposals.novelty: While CRISPR screens for synthetic lethality with kinase inhibitors are common, this approach uniquely targets the system's coordination logic—the docking equilibria—rather than catalytic activity, probing vulnerabilities inherent to the network architecture itself.

proposals.title: Manipulating Competitive Docking of Transcription Factors at Kinase Surfaces as an Evolutionary Trap in Adaptive Cancer Therapy
proposals.motivation: Adaptive therapy, which modulates drug pressure to maintain a stable, treatable tumor population, has shown promise in delaying resistance but lacks molecularly-defined levers for guiding tumor evolution. Concurrently, recent structural atlases (pre-2026) reveal that many kinases share docking surfaces for multiple transcription factors (TFs), creating a competitive intracellular niche. This project aims to bridge these concepts by defining competitive TF docking as a tunable ecological pressure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically adjusting the inhibition of a multi-docking kinase (e.g., a key node in PI3K/mTOR or MAPK pathways) can alter the fitness landscape by favoring tumor subpopulations dependent on one TF-docking axis over another, thereby creating a controllable evolutionary trap that prolongs therapeutic control.
proposals.method: We will first use the pre-2026 kinase-TF interactome atlas and phosphoproteomics to identify a target kinase with multiple, biologically distinct TF docking partners (e.g., ERK2). In isogenic cancer cell lines engineered to be dependent on one specific TF-docking motif (via CRISPR), we will establish competitive co-cultures. Using a microfluidics-based adaptive dosing platform, we will apply a multi-node kinase inhibitor (e.g., a dual mTOR/kinase inhibitor) with dosing schedules informed by a mathematical model that treats TF docking competition as a Lotka-Volterra-type ecological interaction. Single-cell RNA-seq and phospho-flow cytometry will track subpopulation dynamics and pathway rewiring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first proof-of-concept that molecular competition for a shared signaling interface can be harnessed as a therapeutic lever. It will deliver a novel, mechanism-based framework for designing adaptive therapy protocols and identify specific kinase-TF competitive axes as new therapeutic targets.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive therapy typically considers cell-cell competition, this proposal innovates by applying the principle to intracellular molecular competition (TF docking). It directly translates structural biology insights on kinase-TF docking landscapes into a dynamic, ecologically-inspired therapeutic strategy.

proposals.title: Mapping Plastic Kinase Signaling Circuits in Tumor Ecosystems by Integrating High-Dimensional Static and Dynamic Single-Cell Profiling
proposals.motivation: Intratumoral heterogeneity and signaling plasticity are major drivers of therapeutic resistance, but current methods like phospho-flow provide only static, low-dimensional snapshots. A comprehensive understanding of adaptive resistance requires linking the high-dimensional signaling states of individual cells within the tumor ecosystem to their dynamic, functional responses to perturbations, a critical gap pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a deep, static phosphoproteomic atlas of tumor ecosystems with live-cell tracking of key kinase pathway dynamics will reveal plastic, context-dependent signaling circuits that govern cell fate decisions and adaptive resistance to targeted therapies.
proposals.method: We will profile dissociated human tumor samples (e.g., PTEN-deficient cancers) using mass cytometry (CyTOF) with a panel of >40 antibodies targeting phospho-proteins (e.g., pAKT, pERK, pSTAT), drug targets, and lineage markers to construct a static signaling atlas. In parallel, primary cells will be transduced with multiplexed, genetically-encoded FRET biosensors for pathways like AKT and MAPK and cultured in microfluidic devices for controlled drug exposure. Live-cell imaging will track dynamic kinase activity and correlate it with single-cell fates. Data integration will use pre-2026 computational pipelines, including viSNE or PhenoGraph for CyTOF analysis and custom algorithms to map static signaling states to dynamic trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a novel, publicly available resource linking high-dimensional static signaling states to dynamic functional responses in primary human tumors. It will identify specific, plastic kinase circuits that drive adaptive resistance, providing new targets for combination therapies aimed at blocking tumor ecosystem resilience.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration of CyTOF-based deep static phenotyping with live-cell biosensor dynamics in primary tumor ecosystems moves beyond correlative snapshots to directly observe and link signaling states to plastic, adaptive behaviors, a synthesis not achieved in pre-2026 studies.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Profiling Reveals Transcription Factors as Allosteric Modulators of Kinase Networks in PTEN-Heterogeneous Tumors
proposals.motivation: Intratumoral heterogeneity, driven by diverse signaling states, is a major barrier to effective cancer therapy. While transcription factors (TFs) are traditionally viewed as outputs of kinase cascades, recent structural interactome data suggests they may also directly bind and regulate kinases. The functional impact of these interactions on signaling dynamics and drug response within heterogeneous cell populations remains unknown, representing a critical gap in understanding tumor resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific transcription factors, whose expression varies across tumor subpopulations, act as cell-state-specific allosteric regulators of kinase activity, thereby creating distinct signaling niches that drive differential responses to targeted kinase inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will employ high-parameter phospho-flow cytometry (CyTOF) on patient-derived organoids and co-cultures with PTEN-wildtype and -null cells. Panels will simultaneously quantify nuclear TF levels (e.g., STATs, FOXOs, β-catenin) and phosphorylation of key pathway nodes (AKT, ERK, mTORC1 substrates). Following perturbation with PI3K/AKT/mTOR inhibitors, we will apply causal inference algorithms (e.g., SCENERY or single-cell NicheNet adaptations) to the high-dimensional data to infer directional TF→kinase regulatory edges. Putative allosteric TF-kinase pairs will be validated via in vitro kinase assays with recombinant proteins.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first empirical, single-cell map of TF-mediated kinase regulation in cancer, moving beyond correlative associations. It will identify novel, context-dependent resistance mechanisms in heterogeneous tumors and provide a framework for predicting drug response based on TF-driven signaling states.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely inverts the canonical signaling paradigm by functionally testing TFs as upstream allosteric kinase regulators, and does so at single-cell resolution within therapeutically relevant, heterogeneous tumor ecosystems, a concept not yet explored empirically.

proposals.title: A Kinase Network-Based Therapeutic Index Platform for Prioritizing Multi-Node Combination Therapies in Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in targeted kinase inhibitors, their clinical utility is often limited by narrow therapeutic windows due to on-target toxicity in normal tissues or insufficient efficacy from single-agent use. Pre-2026, large-scale pan-cancer screens (e.g., DepMap) and phosphoproteomic kinase activity inference have identified vulnerabilities, but a systematic, integrated framework to predict the therapeutic index of multi-kinase inhibitor combinations from preclinical data is lacking. This gap hinders the rational prioritization of combinations that maximize tumor cell death while sparing critical normal cell functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating pan-cancer efficacy screens with mechanism-based toxicity profiling in primary non-malignant systems will enable the computational prediction of a therapeutic index, successfully identifying kinase inhibitor combinations with optimal efficacy-safety profiles for in vivo validation.
proposals.method: We will first perform a high-throughput screen of kinase inhibitor combinations across the Cancer Cell Line Encyclopedia (CCLE), focusing on nodes within the PI3K/AKT/mTOR and related pathways where PTEN loss creates dependency. In parallel, we will use high-content imaging (Cell Painting) and functional assays (e.g., mitochondrial stress tests) in primary human hepatocyte, cardiomyocyte, and intestinal organoid cultures to quantify mechanism-based toxicities. Data integration will employ a custom scoring algorithm, building on pre-2026 kinase-substrate and docking landscape atlases, to weight efficacy signals against toxicity signatures and rank combinations. Top candidates will be tested in PTEN-deficient murine models with concurrent PDX tumor volume and organ function monitoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, publicly accessible platform for therapeutic index prediction specific to kinase network perturbations. It will generate a ranked list of high-priority combination therapies with annotated mechanistic toxicity risks, providing a resource for the oncology community. The validated in vivo models will offer preclinical proof-of-concept for the top-ranked regimen, accelerating its translational path.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the concurrent, quantitative integration of pan-cancer efficacy and primary system toxicity data into a single predictive index, moving beyond retrospective safety assessment. Leveraging recent kinase-transcription factor interactome and phosphoproteomic inference methods to inform the toxicity mechanisms represents a distinct, systems-pharmacology approach.

proposals.title: Anomaly Detection in Phosphoproteomic Landscapes Identifies Outlier Kinase Activities for Functional Benchmarking
proposals.motivation: Current phosphoproteomic-based kinase activity inference methods, while powerful, are typically validated on known, canonical signaling contexts. This creates a blind spot for identifying kinases with aberrant, context-specific, or non-canonical activity patterns that could be critical in disease states like cancer or in response to novel perturbations. A systematic method to flag these statistical outliers is needed to generate high-value, non-obvious targets for experimental validation and to stress-test the robustness of existing inference algorithms.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying unsupervised anomaly detection to large-scale phosphosite signature databases will robustly identify kinases with statistically deviant inferred activity, revealing novel regulatory nodes and providing a benchmark set for evaluating kinase activity inference methods.
proposals.method: We will integrate large public phosphoproteomic datasets (e.g., from CPTAC) and apply established kinase-substrate relationship databases (e.g., from recent structure-guided interactome atlases) to compute kinase activity scores using established algorithms like Kinase-Substrate Enrichment Analysis (KSEA). We will then apply a multi-algorithm anomaly detection pipeline, including Isolation Forests, One-Class SVMs, and local outlier factor (LOF) analysis, to the resulting kinase activity vectors across samples. Consensus outliers will be prioritized for validation using orthogonal assays, including in vitro kinase profiling and targeted phospho-flow cytometry in relevant cell models (e.g., PTEN-null cancer lines).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first curated atlas of 'kinase activity outliers,' serving as a critical benchmark for the phosphoproteomics community to test and improve inference methods. It will also nominate novel, context-specific kinase regulators for functional follow-up, potentially revealing new therapeutic vulnerabilities, especially in cancers with altered signaling landscapes like those driven by PTEN loss.
proposals.novelty: While kinase activity inference is mature, the systematic application of unsupervised machine learning for outlier detection on these inferred activities is novel. This approach moves beyond comparing known conditions to discovering unanticipated kinase behaviors directly from data patterns, leveraging pre-2026 anomaly detection algorithms in a new biological context.

proposals.title: Mapping Context-Specific Kinase-Substrate Networks in PTEN-Deficient Cancers Using Integrative Phosphoproteomics and Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Current phosphoproteomic analyses often rely on static, context-agnostic kinase-substrate libraries, which fail to capture the dynamic rewiring of signaling networks in different cell types, disease states, or genetic backgrounds. This is a critical limitation for understanding oncogenic signaling driven by kinases in cancers with specific mutations, such as PTEN loss, and for developing targeted therapies. A dynamic, context-aware model is needed to accurately infer kinase activity and identify true functional substrates.
proposals.hypothesis: Kinase-substrate interaction networks are not static but are dynamically rewired in a context-dependent manner, and modeling this rewiring will reveal novel, condition-specific signaling vulnerabilities in PTEN-deficient cancers that are missed by static models.
proposals.method: We will integrate large-scale, publicly available phosphoproteomic datasets from diverse cancer cell lines and patient-derived samples (e.g., from CPTAC) with matched genomic and transcriptomic data. Using a multi-task learning neural network architecture, we will train context-specific models that predict phosphorylation events, where each 'task' represents a distinct cellular context (e.g., PTEN-wildtype vs. PTEN-mutant, different tissue lineages). The model will incorporate known kinase domain specificity (from pre-2026 structural atlases) as a prior and learn context-dependent weighting of potential kinase-substrate pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly accessible, context-aware atlas of kinase-substrate networks for cancer biology. It will provide a computational framework to move beyond static libraries, generate testable hypotheses about condition-specific kinase drivers, and identify novel therapeutic targets tailored to specific genetic backgrounds like PTEN deficiency.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped kinase-substrate interactions or inferred activity, this proposal uniquely focuses on explicitly modeling the *conditional probability* of these interactions as a function of molecular context, moving from a single network to a manifold of possible networks defined by cellular state.

proposals.title: Mapping the Allosteric Substrate Landscape: How Non-Catalytic Domains and Regulatory Subunits Reprogram Kinase Specificity
proposals.motivation: Current phosphoproteomic and kinase activity inference models, while powerful, primarily focus on the catalytic domain's intrinsic sequence preferences. However, kinases in vivo are regulated by complex non-catalytic domains (e.g., SH2, SH3, PH domains) and regulatory subunits (e.g., cyclins, AKAPs), which are critical for cellular signaling fidelity and are frequently mutated in cancer. A systematic understanding of how these elements alter substrate selection beyond the catalytic core is lacking, limiting our ability to predict oncogenic kinase signaling and design specific inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that non-catalytic domains and regulatory subunits impose distinct allosteric constraints and docking interactions that fundamentally reprogram a kinase's intrinsic substrate specificity profile, creating unique "kinase complex" signatures not predictable from catalytic domain sequences alone.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput, structure-guided interactome approach. First, we will generate a library of human kinases fused to their common non-catalytic domains or co-expressed with regulatory subunits. Using a modified, quantitative version of the "kinase–transcription factor interactome" assay, we will profile substrate specificity against a massively parallel peptide library, integrating phosphoproteomic data. We will then develop a new computational pipeline, extending algorithms like NetPhorest and Kinexus, to deconvolute the contribution of non-catalytic elements to phosphorylation site selection, creating a first-of-its-kind "Kinase Complex Substrate Atlas."
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a definitive resource mapping how regulatory architecture dictates kinase signaling output, moving beyond the catalytic domain-centric view. It will provide mechanistic insight into context-specific kinase signaling in health and disease, particularly for kinases like those in the PI3K pathway (relevant to PTEN biology), and offer a new framework for understanding drug resistance and designing next-generation, context-specific kinase therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has cataloged kinase-domain preferences and some docking interactions, this proposal systematically and quantitatively profiles the combined effect of full-length kinase architecture, including regulatory subunits, on global substrate specificity—a key missing layer in the functional annotation of the kinome.

proposals.title: A Structural Atlas of Kinase-Substrate Specificity: Integrating Phosphoproteomic Motifs with Conformational Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Despite comprehensive phosphoproteomic datasets revealing kinase substrate motif preferences, a predictive, mechanistic understanding of specificity remains elusive. This gap hinders the rational design of selective kinase inhibitors and the interpretation of signaling network perturbations in diseases like cancer, where kinases such as those upstream of PTEN are critical. Correlating these motifs with the physical and conformational properties of kinase active sites is a crucial next step to move from correlation to causation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the substrate sequence motif preference of a kinase is directly encoded and predictable from the structural features of its active site, including its conformational state (DFG-in/out, αC-helix orientation) and the physicochemical properties of key binding pockets.
proposals.method: We will integrate pre-2026 resources: 1) Kinase substrate motifs from large-scale phosphoproteomic studies (e.g., Kinase Library) and published datasets, 2) A curated structural database of human kinase domains (from PDB), classified by conformational state using established tools like KLIFS. We will employ machine learning, specifically gradient-boosted tree models (XGBoost) and graph neural networks on residue interaction networks, to learn the mapping between quantifiable structural descriptors (pocket volumes, charge distributions, backbone dynamics from B-factors) and experimentally derived substrate motif logos. Docking simulations using Rosetta will test predictions for specific kinase-transcription factor pairs from existing interactome data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first computationally predictive framework that directly links kinase structure and dynamics to substrate sequence rules. It will generate testable models for kinase-substrate pairing, illuminate the structural basis for functional diversity between eukaryotic and bacterial kinases, and provide a resource for predicting the impact of cancer-associated kinase mutations on signaling specificity.
proposals.novelty: While kinase structures and substrate motifs have been cataloged separately, this proposal uniquely integrates them into a predictive model that accounts for conformational dynamics, moving beyond static sequence alignments. It leverages the growing atlas of kinase-transcription factor complexes to validate predictions in a biologically relevant context.

proposals.title: Evolutionary Coupling Analysis Reveals Co-Evolving Kinase-Substrate Networks in Human Cancer
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of kinase-substrate relationships is a fundamental challenge in cell signaling, with direct implications for understanding oncogenic pathways and designing targeted therapies. Current phosphoproteomic and structural methods, while powerful, often miss context-specific or weak interactions. A complementary, evolution-based approach could uncover conserved functional partnerships that are difficult to detect experimentally.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genuine kinase-substrate pairs exhibit statistically significant patterns of co-evolution across mammalian genomes, and that these evolutionary signatures can be used to predict novel, functionally relevant phosphorylation events, particularly within cancer-associated signaling networks involving kinases like those in the PI3K/AKT pathway downstream of PTEN loss.
proposals.method: We will apply Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) and related co-evolutionary methods, as used successfully for predicting protein-protein interactions and residue contacts from multiple sequence alignments. Using curated kinase and substrate sequence families from pre-2026 databases (PhosphoSitePlus, UniProt), we will construct paired multiple sequence alignments and compute evolutionary couplings. This will be integrated with pre-2026 structural docking landscapes (from Cantley's atlas work) and phosphoproteomic kinase activity inferences to validate and contextualize predicted pairs. Machine learning models (e.g., random forests) will combine co-evolution scores with structural and sequence features for final prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, evolutionarily grounded map of human kinase-substrate interactions, providing a complementary resource to existing atlases. It will generate testable predictions for missing links in oncogenic signaling networks, potentially revealing new substrates for kinases dysregulated in cancer. The method itself will establish a new paradigm for using co-evolution analysis in phospho-signaling.
proposals.novelty: While co-evolution analysis is established in structural biology, its systematic application to predict kinase-substrate pairs across the kinome is novel. This approach moves beyond sequence motif analysis by seeking paired evolutionary trajectories, offering a distinct, genome-wide lens on signaling pathway evolution.

proposals.title: Mapping the Dynamic Kinase-Substrate Interactome in Live Cells Using High-Throughput Protein-Fragment Complementation Assays
proposals.motivation: Current phosphoproteomic and computational inference methods, while powerful, provide static snapshots of kinase activity and lack the ability to capture direct, transient, and context-specific kinase-substrate interactions in living cells. This is a critical gap, as the dynamic rewiring of these interactions underpins cellular responses to stimuli, metabolic shifts, and oncogenic transformation, with direct implications for understanding diseases like PTEN-deficient cancers. A method to directly detect these interactions in vivo is needed to move beyond inference and validate predicted networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-throughput, live-cell protein-fragment complementation assay (PCA) platform, using optimized split-fluorescent or split-luminescent reporters, can systematically and quantitatively map direct, binary kinase-substrate interactions, revealing novel signaling nodes and context-dependent interactions missed by phosphoproteomics alone.
proposals.method: We will implement a high-throughput PCA screen using a split NanoLuc luciferase system. A library of candidate substrate peptides/proteins, derived from phosphoproteomic datasets and structure-guided docking predictions (e.g., from the Kinase–Transcription Factor Interactome Atlas), will be fused to one fragment (SmBiT). A parallel library of kinases, with a focus on the AGC and CAMK families relevant to Cantley's work and PTEN-related signaling, will be fused to the complementary fragment (LgBiT). Interactions will be detected via luminescence in live HEK293T and isogenic PTEN-wild-type/-null cancer cell lines under basal and stimulated conditions. Hits will be validated with orthogonal co-IP and kinase activity assays, and interaction interfaces will be modeled using existing pre-2026 structural databases (PDB) and docking algorithms like HADDOCK.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first large-scale, direct interaction map for kinase-substrate pairs in living cells, providing a crucial functional validation layer for phosphoproteomic inferences. It will identify novel substrates and reveal how oncogenic contexts, such as PTEN loss, rewire proximal signaling networks. The resulting validated interactome will serve as a foundational resource for targeting kinase networks in cancer and other diseases.
proposals.novelty: While PCA is established, its application to systematically screen a kinase library against a substrate library for direct interaction mapping in live cells is novel. This moves beyond inferential methods and static structural atlases to capture the dynamic, cellular-context-dependent kinome-substrate interactome.

proposals.title: Defining the Substrate Recognition Landscapes of Oncogenic and Drug-Resistant Tyrosine Kinase Mutants
proposals.motivation: Oncogenic mutations and acquired resistance mutations in tyrosine kinases (TKs) drive cancer progression and therapy failure, but their precise molecular mechanisms often remain unclear. While kinase activity inference from phosphoproteomics is advancing, a systematic, direct biochemical profiling of how these mutations alter substrate specificity—the core of their signaling output—is lacking. This gap limits our ability to predict downstream pathway rewiring and design effective next-generation inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pathogenic TK mutants possess distinct, quantifiable substrate motif preferences that diverge from their wild-type counterparts, and that these altered specificities directly explain mutant-specific signaling networks and drug resistance mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput, structure-guided peptide library screening platform, building on our established kinase–transcription factor docking atlas methodology. A curated library of degenerate tyrosine-containing peptides will be incubated with purified wild-type and mutant TKs (e.g., EGFR, BCR-ABL, ALK mutants). Phosphorylated substrates will be captured and sequenced via next-generation sequencing. The resulting data will be analyzed using the KINATEST-ID algorithm, a refined version of pre-2026 motif-finding tools, to generate quantitative position-weight matrices for each mutant. Structural insights from pre-2026 kinase–transcription factor docking landscapes will guide mutant selection and interpretation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive atlas of mutant-specific TK substrate preferences, moving beyond simple activity measurements. It will directly link mutant kinase structures to altered signaling outputs, providing a mechanistic rationale for observed phosphoproteomic changes in tumors. The resulting specificity matrices will be a public resource for predicting mutant-driven signaling and for designing mutant-selective inhibitors or combination therapies.
proposals.novelty: While recent work has mapped kinase-substrate interactions or inferred activity, this proposal directly and systematically measures the biophysical alteration in substrate recognition caused by pathogenic mutations, creating a foundational, mechanistic link between genotype and phospho-signaling phenotype.

proposals.title: Computational Design of High-Affinity, High-Specificity Peptide Substrates for the Human Tyrosine Kinome
proposals.motivation: Kinase substrate specificity is central to understanding signaling networks in health and disease, particularly in cancer where kinases like those upstream of PTEN are dysregulated. While phosphoproteomics has enabled inference of kinase activity, the field lacks a systematic, rational approach to design optimal substrates, which are critical tools for probing kinase function, developing biosensors, and informing inhibitor design. A computational design framework would transform our ability to create precise biochemical tools for each kinase.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating existing specificity compendia (e.g., from peptide library screens and structural docking landscapes) with computational protein design algorithms, we can generate novel peptide substrates with predicted higher catalytic efficiency and specificity than known native sequences for each human tyrosine kinase.
proposals.method: We will curate a pre-2026 specificity compendium for tyrosine kinases, integrating data from oriented peptide library screens, phosphoproteomic studies, and the kinase-transcription factor docking atlas. This data will train a discriminative model (e.g., a position-specific scoring matrix or a simple neural network) to predict phosphorylation probability. We will then frame substrate design as an inverse optimization problem, using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) or simulated annealing search over peptide sequence space to maximize the predicted score for a target kinase while minimizing scores for off-target kinases, explicitly modeling specificity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available computational pipeline and a designed substrate library for the tyrosine kinome. It will provide novel, high-quality biochemical tools for the signaling community and validate the principle of inverse design in kinase biochemistry. The resulting substrates will enable more sensitive and specific kinase activity assays.
proposals.novelty: Current approaches identify substrates from natural sequences; here, we propose de novo computational design of optimal, non-natural substrates, treating specificity as a primary, optimizable constraint rather than an observed property. This inverts the standard paradigm of substrate discovery.

proposals.title: Evolutionary and Structural Constraints of Kinase Flanking Domains in Regulating Activation and Substrate Specificity
proposals.motivation: Kinase catalytic domains are well-studied, but the regulatory logic encoded in their flanking domains (e.g., SH2, SH3, PH, and unique N/C-terminal regions) remains poorly understood at a systematic, evolutionary level. Pre-2026 phosphoproteomic and interactome atlases reveal vast signaling networks, yet they often treat kinases as catalytic units without fully integrating the influence of these regulatory modules on pathway specificity and oncogenic activation, particularly in contexts like PTEN-deficient cancers where kinase signaling is rewired.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that kinase flanking domains have co-evolved with their catalytic cores under distinct evolutionary constraints, creating family-specific docking landscapes that allosterically tune activation mechanisms and substrate selection, and that these constraints predict vulnerabilities in disease-associated kinases.
proposals.method: We will integrate pre-2026 structural data (AlphaFold2 models, PDB) with sequence-based evolutionary analysis (ConSurf, Rate4Site) to quantify conservation and co-evolution between catalytic and flanking domains across the human kinome and bacterial serine-threonine kinome. Machine learning models (graph neural networks) will be trained on kinase-transcription factor docking landscapes and phosphoproteomic-based activity data to predict how specific flanking domain features influence substrate phosphorylation profiles and pathway output. Functional validation will use chimeric kinase constructs and substrate-trapping mutants in cellular models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a predictive map linking kinase regulatory domain architecture to functional output, explaining family-specific signaling rules. It will identify novel allosteric regulatory sites, offering new targets for selective kinase inhibition, especially in cancers with altered signaling networks like those involving PTEN loss.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies cataloged kinase interactions or structures in isolation, this proposal uniquely applies evolutionary constraint analysis directly to the question of how non-catalytic domains mechanistically shape kinase function across kingdoms, integrating structural prediction with functional network data to derive general design principles.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Network Model of Serine-Threonine Kinase (STK) Signaling Reveals Context-Specific PTEN-Regulated Pathways in Cancer
proposals.motivation: Current kinase biology relies heavily on static family classifications and static interaction atlases, which fail to capture the dynamic, context-dependent rewiring of signaling networks that drives cellular decision-making and disease. This is a critical limitation in cancer, where oncogenic mutations and tumor suppressors like PTEN alter kinase-substrate relationships and network topology in ways that static maps cannot predict, hindering the development of effective, context-aware therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling STK signaling as a dynamic, condition-specific interaction network, rather than a static kinome, will reveal novel, PTEN-regulated signaling modules and conserved topological motifs that dictate oncogenic outcomes and therapeutic vulnerabilities.
proposals.method: We will integrate pre-2026 resources: the kinase-transcription factor docking atlas for structural interaction potentials, large-scale phosphoproteomic datasets (including PTEN-wildtype and -null contexts) for kinase activity inference, and bacterial STK diversity data for evolutionary constraints. We will construct dynamic networks using a graph neural network (GNN) architecture trained to predict context-specific edge weights (interaction strengths) from multi-omic features (expression, mutation, phosphorylation state). The model will be validated using perturbation data (kinase inhibitors, PTEN modulation) to test its predictive power for network rewiring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational framework to move beyond static kinome maps to predictive, dynamic network models of signaling. It will identify new PTEN-dependent kinase modules and network vulnerabilities, offering a systems-level understanding of how PTEN loss reconfigures the signaling landscape to drive cancer progression and resistance.
proposals.novelty: While atlases of kinase interactions exist, this proposal uniquely integrates structural docking data, evolutionary insights, and phosphoproteomics into a dynamic, learnable graph model that predicts network state from cellular context, a significant advance over current static classifications.

ideas.title: Predictive sequence-embedding modeling of adaptive kinase-TF rewiring under inverted PI3K-mTOR targeting
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the inverted targeting strategy (mTOR then PI3Kα inhibition) with protein language model-based interface featurization to predict and validate adaptive kinase-TF interactions that are uniquely created or exposed by the drug sequence. The core hypothesis is that the mTOR-first inhibition induces a specific conformational and signaling rewiring, and that the evolutionary and physicochemical patterns captured in protein language model embeddings can identify which transcription factor interfaces become newly accessible or critical for PI3Kα to engage as a compensatory survival mechanism. By featurizing these predicted, drug sequence-induced interfaces, we can computationally prioritize the most therapeutically relevant TF targets for subsequent combinatorial disruption, transforming the empirical 'inversion' test into a principled, predictive model of dynamic network adaptation.

ideas.title: Systematically probe understudied kinase therapeutic potential
ideas.description: Functionally characterize the understudied kinases identified as potential cancer targets through drug screening or genetic perturbation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deciphering the dark cancer phosphoproteome using machine-learned co-regulation of phosphosites

ideas.title: Integrate specificity with structural models of kinases
ideas.description: Correlate substrate motif preferences with kinase active site structures and conformational states.

ideas.title: Create a dynamic, condition-specific interaction atlas
ideas.description: Integrate the structural atlas with expression data to model context-specific kinase-TF networks across tissues and diseases.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Structure-Guided Kinase–Transcription Factor Interactome Atlas Reveals Docking Landscapes of the Kinome

ideas.title: Single-Cell Mapping of Transcription Factor-Mediated Allosteric Kinase Regulation in Tumor Heterogeneity
ideas.description: This research direction combines high-dimensional phospho-flow cytometry with the novel hypothesis that transcription factors (TFs) can allosterically regulate kinase activity. We will profile patient-derived tumor samples or complex co-culture models to simultaneously quantify the expression/activation states of specific TFs and the phosphorylation states of key signaling pathway nodes at single-cell resolution. By applying targeted kinase inhibitors or stimuli and analyzing correlative and causal relationships between TF states and phospho-profiles across heterogeneous cell populations, we aim to empirically identify which TFs function as context-dependent allosteric regulators of kinase networks. This approach moves beyond viewing TFs solely as pathway outputs to mapping them as active, cell-state-specific modulators that drive differential drug responses within tumors.

ideas.title: Integrate additional omics layers for co-regulation
ideas.description: Incorporate proteomics or transcriptomics data to refine the phosphosite co-regulation network and kinase predictions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deciphering the dark cancer phosphoproteome using machine-learned co-regulation of phosphosites

ideas.title: Cross-species mechanistic dissection of 4E-BP1 suppression efficacy and resistance via conserved and divergent docking interfaces
ideas.description: This study integrates a mechanistic investigation of 4E-BP1 suppression with a systematic cross-species comparison of its regulatory interfaces. We will experimentally challenge the functional conservation of predicted docking interactions for 4E-BP1 and its kinases (like mTORC1) between human cancer models and Drosophila, using genetic and pharmacological suppression. By correlating the degree of conservation of specific molecular interfaces with the phenotypic consequences of suppression—on translation rates, cell proliferation, and tumor growth—we can pinpoint which mechanistic nodes are essential and evolutionarily hardened versus those that are plastic and potentially compensatory. This approach will reveal fundamental principles of target vulnerability and predict which aspects of 4E-BP1 inhibition are most likely to yield robust, non-resistance-prone anticancer effects.

ideas.title: Sequential Pathway Priming: Inverting PI3K-mTOR Inhibition Order
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from antibiotic stewardship for biofilm infections, where a matrix-disrupting agent is used first to prime the system for a lethal antibiotic, this idea inverts the node targeting order in the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway. The analogous solution involves a sequential 'priming' strategy to break down defenses before delivering the primary lethal blow. Adapted to cancer signaling, the research idea is to test if inhibiting the downstream integrator mTOR first disrupts critical feedback and homeostatic mechanisms, thereby 'priming' the cellular state and making it fundamentally more vulnerable to a subsequent strike against the upstream kinase PI3Kα. This sequential inhibition (mTOR then PI3Kα) is hypothesized to be more effective by preventing compensatory survival signals and inducing synergistic cell death or durable pathway suppression.

ideas.title: Allosteric Docking-Site Inhibitors for Selective PI3Kα/mTORC1/mTORC2 Combination Therapy
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to rationally design a triple-drug combination targeting the PI3Kα-mTOR pathway, but with a novel mechanism. Instead of using standard ATP-competitive inhibitors for PI3Kα, mTORC1, and mTORC2, we will develop allosteric modulators that specifically block the docking surfaces and interaction hotspots required for substrate recruitment and complex assembly. For example, an allosteric agent could prevent specific substrates from docking with mTORC1 or mTORC2, or disrupt the critical Rictor-mTOR interaction for mTORC2-specific inhibition. This approach aims to yield a combination therapy with superior pathway selectivity, reduced off-target effects, and the potential to overcome resistance mechanisms seen with traditional catalytic site inhibitors.

ideas.title: Map specificity of pathogenic kinase mutants
ideas.description: Profile substrate motif preferences of oncogenic and drug-resistant tyrosine kinase mutants.
ideas.sourceTitle: The intrinsic substrate specificity of the human tyrosine kinome

ideas.title: Biomarker-driven modular trial of multi-node inhibition with adaptive combination therapy
ideas.description: This redesigned approach initiates a coordinated preclinical and clinical program to rationally translate the PI3Kα-mTOR-ISD inhibition concept. First, a large bank of molecularly annotated patient-derived organoids (PDOs) is used to exhaustively map the genetic and transcriptomic contexts required for synergistic lethality, defining a predictive biomarker signature. Clinically, this informs a modular, adaptive phase 1b trial where all patients receive the dual PI3Kα/mTOR inhibitor backbone, but the ISD inhibitor is added only for those patients whose tumors, via sequential biopsy, demonstrate persistent spliceosome vulnerability or activation of the target pathway. This biomarker-adaptive design maximizes safety, validates the mechanistic hypothesis in humans, and identifies the precise patient population for subsequent efficacy trials.

ideas.title: Relax the assumption of static kinase-substrate relationships
ideas.description: Model context-specific kinase-substrate networks that change across cell types, conditions, or disease states instead of using static libraries.

ideas.description: Investigate if transcription factors can allosterically regulate kinase activity, not just be phosphorylation substrates.

ideas.title: Study evolutionary constraints in regulatory domains
ideas.description: Analyze how flanking domains influence kinase activation and specificity across families.

ideas.title: Dynamic, High-Dimensional Single-Cell Signaling Network Profiling in Tumor Ecosystems
ideas.description: This redesigned approach combines two complementary, high-content single-cell technologies to overcome the static and limited nature of standard phospho-flow. First, mass cytometry (CyTOF) is used on dissociated tumor samples to provide a deep, static map of >40 signaling phospho-proteins, drug targets, and lineage markers across millions of cells, defining heterogeneous signaling states. In parallel, primary tumor cells are transduced with multiplexed FRET- or translocation-based biosensors and cultured in microfluidic devices, enabling live-cell imaging to track the dynamic activity of key pathways (e.g., MAPK, AKT, STAT) and correlate them with functional cell fate decisions in real time following drug exposure. By linking the high-dimensional static atlas with dynamic functional data, the system identifies not just heterogeneous states, but the plastic signaling circuits that drive adaptive resistance.

ideas.title: Systematic mapping of translation regulator phosphorylation by non-canonical kinase partners to identify novel therapeutic vulnerabilities
ideas.description: This project fuses the iLIS interaction mapping framework with a focused mechanistic study on translation suppression. We will first apply the expanded iLIS platform to comprehensively map interactions between kinases and families of direct translation regulators, including 4E-BP1 and other eIF4E-binding proteins, eIF4G isoforms, and PDCD4. This interaction atlas will then be functionally interrogated to determine how phosphorylation by these identified, potentially non-canonical kinase partners mechanistically controls translation efficiency, ribosome loading, and specific mRNA translation. The ultimate goal is to identify and validate novel kinase-translation regulator axes whose inhibition synergizes with or surpasses known 4E-BP1-targeting strategies to suppress oncogenic translation and tumor growth.

ideas.title: Scale down to study single-cell kinase-TF interaction variability
ideas.description: Use the atlas to infer cell-to-cell heterogeneity in kinase signaling networks via single-cell proteomics.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Structure-Guided Kinase–Transcription Factor Interactome Atlas Reveals Docking Landscapes of the Kinome

ideas.title: Modular kinase blockade via selective, temporally-controlled inhibitors
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a single dual mTORC1/mTORC2 inhibitor is necessary or optimal for targeting the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway in cancer. Drawing an analogy from precision agriculture and integrated pest management (IPM), which solves the problem of pest resistance and ecosystem damage by replacing broad-spectrum pesticides with a combination of selective, timed interventions. The analogous IPM solution is adapted by proposing a modular kinase blockade: a PI3Kα inhibitor forms the foundation, but separate, selective mTORC1 and mTORC2 inhibitors are used in a temporally-controlled manner. Administration would be guided by real-time biomarker feedback (e.g., phosphorylation status of downstream targets), allowing for sequential or pulsed inhibition of each complex to dynamically counteract the dominant survival signal and minimize pathway compensatory mechanisms and off-target effects associated with continuous, concurrent pan-mTOR inhibition.

ideas.title: Transfer protein language model features
ideas.description: Incorporate embeddings from protein language models as additional features for predicting kinase binding specificity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deciphering the dark cancer phosphoproteome using machine-learned co-regulation of phosphosites

ideas.title: Dual problem: Exploit pathway hyperactivation for synthetic lethality
ideas.description: Identify vulnerabilities specific to tumours with PI3-K pathway hyperactivation, even without driver mutations, for synthetic lethal targeting.
ideas.sourceTitle: Paediatric strategy forum for medicinal product development of PI3-K, mTOR, AKT and GSK3β inhibitors in children and adolescents with cancer.

ideas.title: Transcription Factors as Allosteric Kinase Modulators: A Reverse-Signaling Paradigm
ideas.description: This idea inverts the canonical kinase-transcription factor regulatory direction by proposing that transcription factors can allosterically regulate kinase activity, not just be phosphorylation substrates. The analogy is drawn from enzymology and allosteric regulation, where the solution to understanding non-substrate modulators involved identifying allosteric binding pockets and characterizing the induced conformational changes. Adapted to this context, the research would systematically investigate TFs as potential allosteric kinase modulators. This involves screening for physical TF-kinase interactions outside the kinase active site, using techniques like X-ray crystallography or cryo-EM to identify allosteric binding interfaces, and employing kinetic assays to measure changes in kinase activity (Vmax, Km) upon TF binding. Success would reveal a novel layer of signaling complexity where TFs provide direct feedback to upstream kinases, challenging the linearity of traditional signaling pathways.

ideas.title: Expand profiling to include non-catalytic domain influences
ideas.description: Investigate how kinase domains outside the catalytic core or regulatory subunits alter intrinsic substrate specificity.
ideas.sourceTitle: The intrinsic substrate specificity of the human tyrosine kinome

ideas.title: CRISPR screening for synthetic lethality induced by competitive kinase docking interference
ideas.description: This research direction applies high-throughput CRISPR screening to identify genetic vulnerabilities that become lethal only when cells are subjected to perturbations that enforce competitive, rather than cooperative, docking at key signaling nodes like kinases. We will engineer cells where specific transcription factor docking motifs are decoyed or saturated, forcing competition for kinase binding surfaces, and then perform genome-wide knockout screens to find genes essential for survival under this constrained signaling regime. This approach moves beyond identifying vulnerabilities to simple kinase inhibition by probing the system's fragility when its inherent buffering capacity—enabled by cooperative or multiplexed docking—is deliberately compromised. The expected output is a new class of synthetic lethal targets specific to the dysregulation of competitive docking equilibria, with implications for targeting oncogenic signaling pathways that rely on such multiplexed interactions.

ideas.title: Apply co-evolution analysis to kinase-substrate prediction
ideas.description: Use evolutionary coupling methods from structural biology to identify co-evolving kinase-substrate pairs.
ideas.sourceTitle: The intrinsic substrate specificity of the human tyrosine kinome

ideas.title: Use protein language model embeddings to predict patient-specific multi-node drug synergy for clinical trial optimization
ideas.description: This research direction integrates protein language model (pLM) embeddings to predict kinase-TF and other critical signaling interfaces disrupted by drugs like serabelisib and sapanisertib. By modeling these interfaces across patient-derived tumor genomic profiles, the approach predicts which specific multi-node combinations (e.g., serabelisib-sapanisertib-ISD) will be most synergistic for individual patients or molecular subtypes of endometrial and breast cancer. This enables the design of biomarker-stratified, adaptive clinical trials where patient cohorts are assigned to the most rationally predicted drug combination based on their tumor's inferred interface vulnerability landscape, thereby increasing trial efficiency and therapeutic precision.

ideas.title: Invert kinase-centric to phosphatase-centric view
ideas.description: Build a predictive model for phosphatase-substrate associations instead of focusing solely on kinases.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deciphering the dark cancer phosphoproteome using machine-learned co-regulation of phosphosites

ideas.title: Explore combination with other targeted therapies
ideas.description: Systematically test the multi-node backbone with additional hormone therapies and CDK4/6 inhibitors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multi-node inhibition targeting mTORC1, mTORC2 and PI3Kα potently inhibits the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway in endometrial and breast cancer models

ideas.title: Evolutionary conservation of PI3K-mTOR pathway synthetic lethality informs intermittent dosing strategies
ideas.description: This project investigates whether the synthetic lethality of concurrent PI3Kα and mTOR inhibition is conserved in Drosophila, leveraging its genetic tractability to systematically map the functional conservation of key docking interactions within this pathway. By identifying which specific protein-protein interfaces are essential for the synergistic toxicity across species, we can distinguish core, evolutionarily constrained vulnerabilities from species-specific or dispensable interactions. This evolutionary insight then directly informs the design of sequential or intermittent dosing regimens in mammalian models, specifically targeting only the conserved, essential nodes of interaction to maintain anti-tumor efficacy. Thus, the cross-species comparison provides a principled, mechanism-based rationale for de-escalating therapy to reduce toxicity, rather than empirical dose scheduling.

ideas.title: Develop LF activators as research tools
ideas.description: Seek small molecules that enhance LF activity to study MAPKK signaling pathways and cell death.
ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of small molecule inhibitors of anthrax lethal factor

ideas.title: Decoupling sequence from duration using orthogonal mTOR reactivation
ideas.description: This redesigned study directly tests the importance of inhibition sequence by eliminating the confounding variable of differential mTOR inhibitor exposure time. It employs cells engineered with a rapamycin-resistant, chemically inducible mTOR variant (e.g., fused to a destabilizing domain). After initial mTOR inhibition with rapamycin, the orthogonal mTOR is acutely stabilized with a small molecule to reactivate mTOR signaling, creating a defined 'mTOR-ON' state. Adding a PI3Kα inhibitor at this precise moment tests the 'mTOR-first, then PI3Kα' sequence against controls with matched rapamycin duration but different sequences, isolating the pure effect of nodal targeting order on cell fate and signaling adaptation.

ideas.title: Apply combinatorial stressor screening from microbial ecology to identify genetic dependencies in multi-node inhibited cells
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from microbial ecology, where high-throughput screens under combinatorial chemical stress identify microbes with unique vulnerabilities, this idea adapts that solution to cancer biology. The analogous problem of finding specific sensitivities only under multi-inhibitor conditions is solved by performing the functional genetic screen (CRISPR knockout) in an environment pre-conditioned with a fixed combination of pathway inhibitors. This adapted approach will systematically uncover genetic dependencies that are exclusive to the poly-pharmacological state, identifying potent and context-specific synthetic lethal interactions for targeted combination therapies in cancer.

ideas.title: Predicting Tumor Escape via Co-evolutionary Analysis of Resistance-Inducing Allosteric Mutations
ideas.description: This research direction applies co-evolution analysis (DCA) to large sequence alignments of oncogenic signaling proteins to identify networks of residues that constitute latent allosteric hotspots. By modeling how these co-evolving networks are perturbed under the selective pressure of multi-node inhibitor combinations, the project predicts the specific allosteric mutations tumors are most likely to acquire to rewire signaling and evade therapy. This computational prediction is then validated experimentally in cell line and patient-derived models treated with combination therapies, directly linking predicted co-evolutionary signatures to observed resistance mechanisms. The outcome is a novel predictive tool for anticipating clinical resistance, guiding the design of next-generation inhibitors or drug combinations that preemptively target these forecasted escape routes.

ideas.title: Mechanistic study of 4E-BP1 suppression impact
ideas.description: Investigate the functional consequences of superior 4E-BP1 phosphorylation inhibition on translation and tumor growth.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multi-node inhibition targeting mTORC1, mTORC2 and PI3Kα potently inhibits the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway in endometrial and breast cancer models

ideas.title: Target LF domains beyond the catalytic site
ideas.description: Seek inhibitors that bind allosteric sites on LF rather than competing at the Zn-dependent active site.
ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of small molecule inhibitors of anthrax lethal factor

ideas.title: Pan-Cancer Allosteric Vulnerability Mapping via Co-evolutionary Signatures
ideas.description: This project integrates direct coupling analysis (DCA) for predicting allosteric hotspots with a large-scale, pan-cancer efficacy screen. First, DCA is applied to sequence alignments of key oncoproteins to computationally identify co-evolving residue networks indicative of allosteric communication. These predicted allosteric hotspots are then systematically perturbed—using CRISPRi or small-molecule probes—across a diverse panel of cancer cell lines to map their functional necessity. The resulting pan-cancer functional data validates the computational predictions and reveals which allosteric networks are critical in specific cancer lineages or genetic backgrounds, generating a prioritized map of targetable allosteric vulnerabilities.

ideas.title: Define molecular link between stemness and OXPHOS
ideas.description: Investigate the specific molecular mechanisms connecting the cancer stem cell state to increased oxidative phosphorylation dependence in surviving cells.
ideas.sourceTitle: Oncogene ablation-resistant pancreatic cancer cells depend on mitochondrial function

ideas.title: Apply chemical proteomics to map inhibitor selectivity
ideas.description: Use chemical proteomic profiling to comprehensively define the on- and off-target effects of PI3-K/mTOR inhibitors in paediatric models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Paediatric strategy forum for medicinal product development of PI3-K, mTOR, AKT and GSK3β inhibitors in children and adolescents with cancer.

ideas.title: Model competitive docking as a ligand-receptor binding equilibrium with steric exclusion
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of cooperative docking by modeling it as a competitive, zero-sum process. Drawing an analogy from pharmacology, where multiple drugs compete for a single receptor's binding site, we adapt the framework of competitive ligand-receptor equilibrium. The kinase's docking surface is treated as a receptor with a defined site capacity. The docking motifs from different transcription factors (TFs) are treated as ligands with specific dissociation constants (Kd) for that site. By applying the law of mass action, we can computationally model the fractional occupancy of the kinase by each TF as a function of their intracellular concentrations and relative affinities. This adapted approach allows us to predict signaling outcomes—such as which TF gets phosphorylated and activates downstream genes—based on quantitative principles of competition, moving beyond a qualitative cooperative model.

ideas.title: Quantifying competitive docking in live cells via orthogonal FRET-based displacement assays
ideas.description: This redesigned system directly tests the competition hypothesis by engineering a synthetic, modular kinase-docking circuit in live cells. Orthogonal fluorescent protein pairs are used to create separate FRET sensors for the interaction between a single kinase and each putative competing docking motif. By monitoring FRET signals in real-time upon perturbation (e.g., induced expression of a competitor motif), the system quantifies direct displacement at the kinase surface, distinguishing it from indirect network effects. This approach transforms the conceptual model into a falsifiable, quantitative assay for competitive docking dynamics within a native cellular environment.

ideas.title: Challenge AlphaFold-Multimer's accuracy for weak/transient interactions
ideas.description: Test if the iLIS framework fails for low-affinity or highly dynamic kinase-docking interactions not well-modeled by AF2.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Structure-Guided Kinase–Transcription Factor Interactome Atlas Reveals Docking Landscapes of the Kinome

ideas.title: Causal dissection of acute 4E-BP1 dephosphorylation on translational reprogramming and tumor maintenance
ideas.description: This study utilizes a chemogenetically controlled, acute 4E-BP1 dephosphorylation system (e.g., a rapamycin-inducible phosphatase fused to 4E-BP1) to establish direct causality, bypassing chronic pharmacological inhibition. Immediate, genome-wide translational changes are quantified via ribosome profiling and pulsed SILAC, pinpointing the specific mRNA cohorts whose translation is 4E-BP1-dependent. This precise translational signature is then functionally validated by integrating the inducible system into autochthonous or patient-derived tumor models, testing the causal requirement of these specific translated proteins for tumor growth and survival in vivo.

ideas.title: Solve inverse problem: phosphosite to pathway activity
ideas.description: Use the co-regulation network to infer upstream pathway activities from observed phosphosite phosphorylation patterns.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deciphering the dark cancer phosphoproteome using machine-learned co-regulation of phosphosites

ideas.title: Competitive Docking as a Tunable Ecological Pressure in Adaptive Cancer Therapy
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to model and exploit competitive docking between transcription factors (TFs) at a shared kinase surface as a novel, molecularly-defined ecological niche within a tumor. By applying adaptive therapy principles, the dosing of a multi-node inhibitor (e.g., a kinase inhibitor) is dynamically adjusted not just to reduce tumor burden, but to strategically alter the fitness landscape: favoring tumor cell subpopulations dependent on one TF docking motif while suppressing those dependent on another. This creates an evolutionary trap, where therapy-guided competition between intracellular signaling pathways becomes a driver of controllable tumor evolution, potentially delaying resistance by maintaining a manageable, less aggressive population. The synergy lies in treating molecular competition as an ecological parameter to be manipulated, rather than just a static feature of signaling networks.

ideas.title: Challenge that more molecular data always improves prognosis
ideas.description: Test if simpler, cheaper models using limited data can match complex molecular profiling for prognosis.
ideas.sourceTitle: Advances in breast cancer research.

ideas.description: Employ knowledge graph embedding techniques to integrate diverse biological databases for improved kinase-substrate association prediction.

ideas.title: Integrated pan-cancer efficacy and mechanistic toxicity screen for therapeutic index
ideas.description: This redesigned approach first executes the pan-cancer cell line screen to identify broadly effective multi-node combinations. In parallel, it employs high-content imaging and functional assays in primary non-malignant cell and organoid systems to predict mechanism-based toxicities. The data streams are integrated using a scoring algorithm that ranks combinations by their predicted therapeutic window. The top-ranked candidates are then advanced into a streamlined in vivo proof-of-concept study that concurrently measures tumor regression and key organ toxicities, ensuring only combinations with a viable efficacy-safety profile move forward.

ideas.title: Closed-loop, model-informed biomarker discovery for adaptive dosing
ideas.description: This research direction integrates computational modeling of pathway dynamics with biomarker discovery to create an adaptive, patient-specific dosing system. Mathematical models simulate feedback loops to predict therapeutic windows, while concurrently, multi-omics data is analyzed to identify early biomarkers signaling a trajectory toward excessive inhibition. The synergy emerges by using the model to guide biomarker search in silico (e.g., predicting which pathway node's dynamics best correlate with impending toxicity) and then using the discovered biomarkers to calibrate and personalize the model parameters. This creates a virtuous cycle where models become more predictive of individual toxicity risks, enabling real-time dose adjustments that proactively avoid adverse events while maintaining efficacy.

ideas.title: Study evolutionary divergence of specificity in detail
ideas.description: Compare intrinsic kinase specificities across key metazoan nodes to trace functional diversification.
ideas.sourceTitle: The intrinsic substrate specificity of the human tyrosine kinome

ideas.title: Applying Network Flow Control Principles to Map Translation Reprogramming Under 4E-BP1 Suppression
ideas.description: This research seed proposes a mechanistic study of the functional consequences of superior 4E-BP1 phosphorylation inhibition. Drawing an analogy from telecommunications network engineering, where the impact of a new traffic-shaping policy is understood through deep packet inspection and flow analysis—not just aggregate throughput—this idea adapts that solution. The adapted research approach is to use high-resolution translational profiling techniques (Ribo-seq, polysome analysis) as 'deep inspection' tools. The goal is to systematically map how potent 4E-BP1 suppression reprograms the translational 'network,' identifying which specific mRNA transcripts and oncogenic pathways see the greatest changes in ribosome 'traffic' (translation efficiency), and to discover new translational bottlenecks that emerge, thereby providing a detailed circuit-level understanding of its impact on tumor growth beyond bulk protein synthesis measurements.

ideas.title: Evaluate resistance mechanisms to multi-node inhibition
ideas.description: Identify how tumors eventually evade this potent combination therapy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multi-node inhibition targeting mTORC1, mTORC2 and PI3Kα potently inhibits the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway in endometrial and breast cancer models

ideas.title: Integrate language model embeddings with geometric deep learning for kinase-TF interface prediction
ideas.description: This approach synergistically combines the evolutionary insights from protein language model embeddings with explicit three-dimensional structural information. A graph neural network is constructed from predicted or modeled kinase-TF complexes, using residues as nodes (featurized with pLM embeddings) and spatial contacts as edges. This architecture allows the model to learn the precise geometric and physicochemical patterns that define a stable interface, moving beyond sequence statistics alone. The resulting system is designed to score interaction likelihoods based on both evolutionary fitness and physical plausibility, leading to more robust and accurate predictions.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Signaling Topography via Barcoded Phospho-Flow
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and spatial ecology to the problem of analyzing signaling heterogeneity in tumors. In ecology, individual animals are tagged (barcoded) and their movement/environmental data is tracked, then aggregated and mapped to understand population-level spatial patterns. Here, we propose dissecting a tumor into distinct topographic regions (e.g., core, periphery). Cells from each region are labeled with a unique mass or fluorescent barcode, then pooled and processed together in a single phospho-flow cytometry run to assess pathway inhibition states. The barcodes allow the high-dimensional single-cell signaling data to be computationally assigned back to its spatial origin after acquisition. This creates a spatially-resolved, single-cell 'signaling topography' of the tumor, revealing how the efficacy of a pathway inhibitor varies across different microenvironments within the same tumor mass.

ideas.title: Adaptive clinical trial design using a synthetic control arm from real-world data
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting the hardware-in-the-loop testing solution from aerospace engineering to the clinical trial context. The analogous problem—safely and efficiently testing a complex, multi-input control system for an unstable platform—is solved using a high-fidelity digital simulation that interacts with the physical hardware. Translated to oncology: for the serabelisib-sapanisertib-ISD combination trial in endometrial and breast cancer, we would construct a synthetic control arm from deep, curated real-world data to act as the 'digital twin' of the disease population. This adaptive design supplements a small concurrent control arm, enabling a more powerful and efficient test of the multi-node inhibition hypothesis. It allows for dynamic comparison, potentially requiring fewer patients randomized to placebo/standard care, providing a robust, ethical, and accelerated pathway to evaluate the clinical impact of simultaneously targeting PI3K, mTOR, and IR/IGF-1R pathways.

ideas.title: Quantifying the metabolic rewiring induced by pharmacological insulin suppression in cancer therapy
ideas.description: This research direction employs metabolic flux analysis (MFA) with isotopologue tracing to systematically quantify how pharmacological suppression of insulin—using agents like SGLT2 inhibitors or metformin—rewires central carbon metabolism in cancer cells. It directly tests the hypothesis that a drug-induced low-insulin state creates distinct metabolic dependencies compared to a diet-induced state, potentially identifying unique therapeutic nodes. By applying MFA to cells under pharmacological insulin suppression, we can pinpoint compensatory metabolic fluxes that sustain tumor growth, revealing new targets for rational drug combinations that exploit this specific metabolic environment.

ideas.title: Priming Cancer Cells for Targeted Eradication via Transient Pathway Hyperactivation
ideas.description: This idea uses analogical reasoning from immunology/vaccinology, specifically the 'prime-boost' strategy used to sensitize and amplify immune responses. The analogous solution involves an initial antigen exposure to prime specific T-cell clones, followed by a boost to trigger a potent, targeted eradication. Adapted to the original seed idea on pathway hyperactivation, the research would investigate if transiently hyperactivating an oncogenic pathway (the 'prime') in cancer cells creates a sensitized, vulnerable state. This would be followed by targeted pathway inhibition (the 'boost'), with the goal of achieving dramatically enhanced, synergistic cell killing compared to inhibition alone, effectively solving the inverse problem by using hyperactivation to create a therapeutic window for more effective subsequent inhibition.
