ideas.title: Dynamic Network Trajectory Mapping to Preempt Adaptive Resistance in Combination Therapy
ideas.description: This redesigned approach systematically interrogates how the multi-node signaling backbone evolves dynamically under pressure from hormone therapies and CDK4/6 inhibitors, rather than just testing static combinations. It employs longitudinal, single-cell multi-omics profiling to map adaptive signaling rewiring in real time, identifying critical nodes that drive escape. These data feed into a predictive network model that nominates second-node interventions designed to block resistance pathways before they consolidate. The final validation involves testing these preemptive, model-informed double or triple combinations not just for initial efficacy, but for their ability to delay or prevent relapse in persistent treatment models.

ideas.title: Feedback-Contingent Pathway Priming: Stratifying Sensitization by Real-Time Adaptation Signatures
ideas.description: This study investigates whether transient pathway hyperactivation sensitizes cells to inhibition by explicitly controlling for the confounding effect of adaptive feedback. A live-cell, dual-fluorescence reporter system is implemented to monitor, in real time, both the direct output of the target pathway and the induction of its key endogenous negative regulators during a precisely timed hyperactivation pulse. Cells are then sorted based on their adaptation signature—specifically selecting those with high pathway output but low feedback induction—before challenging them with pathway inhibition. This approach directly tests the core hypothesis by isolating the sensitization effect from the prevalent mechanism of feedback-driven desensitization, yielding a more robust and predictive model for therapeutic priming strategies.

ideas.title: Causally link Sgg/GSK3-Hnf4 phosphorylation to lipid flux in vivo using inducible phospho-alleles and metabolic tracing
ideas.description: This study will directly test the causal role of Sgg/GSK3 phosphorylation on Hnf4 function in lipid metabolism by generating and analyzing knock-in Drosophila models expressing inducible, tissue-specific phospho-mimetic (S/T to D/E) and phospho-defective (S/T to A) Hnf4 alleles. Using these genetic tools in the adult fat body, we will employ stable isotope tracing (e.g., 13C-glucose or 13C-fatty acids) coupled with mass spectrometry to quantitatively measure the flux through lipogenesis, lipid oxidation, and lipoprotein transport pathways in real time. This approach will definitively establish whether altering this specific post-translational modification is sufficient to rewire metabolic networks, moving beyond descriptive target identification to a mechanistic, causal understanding of the regulatory node's physiological impact.

ideas.title: Targeted in situ localization and sub-tomogram averaging of kinase-docking complexes via genetically encoded proximity labeling
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the 'needle-in-a-haystack' problem by combining cellular cryo-ET with targeted proximity labeling. A kinase of interest is fused to a minimal engineered peroxidase (APEX2), which, in vitrified cells, catalyzes the deposition of biotin-phenol radicals within a ~20 nm radius. Subsequent cryo-FIB milling, on-grid labeling with streptavidin-conjugated gold nanoparticles, and cryo-ET direct data collection to regions harboring the kinase. Sub-tomogram averaging is then performed specifically on macromolecular densities adjacent to gold fiducials, enabling the structural identification of transient kinase-transcription factor docking complexes within their native cellular environment.

ideas.title: Applying a Neurobiology Circuit-Mapping Approach to Dissect the Sgg/GSK3-Hnf4 Lipid Regulatory Network
ideas.description: This idea proposes to investigate the downstream targets and physiological consequences of the Sgg/GSK3-Hnf4 regulatory mechanism by drawing an analogy from systems neuroscience. In connectomics, the function of a neural circuit is dissected by precisely mapping its components and their connections through targeted genetic manipulations and observing the resulting behavioral output. Adapted to this lipid metabolism context, the research would employ a similar strategy of intersectional genetics in vivo—using tissue-specific drivers and temporal gene switches (e.g., Gal4/UAS, TARGET, or CRISPRi/a) to selectively manipulate Sgg/GSK3 activity, Hnf4 expression, or candidate intermediary factors in specific metabolic tissues. The 'circuit' output would then be mapped by comprehensively analyzing subsequent changes in the transcriptional network (via RNA-seq), lipidomic profiles, and key physiological metrics like energy storage, mobilization, and organismal fitness under metabolic challenge. This approach aims to move beyond correlation to establish causal, tissue-resolved links within the Sgg/GSK3-Hnf4 regulatory module, defining its role in systemic metabolic homeostasis.

ideas.title: Adaptive Combination Therapy Testing Using a Network Stability Framework
ideas.description: Drawing from ecological network stability research, this adapted idea proposes to systematically test combination therapies using a framework from ecosystem resilience studies. In ecology, scientists test food web stability by perturbing multiple keystone species in sequence and combination, measuring how the network compensates and maintains function. Adapted to cancer therapeutics, this approach would involve applying the multi-node backbone therapy with additional hormone therapies and CDK4/6 inhibitors not as simple combinations, but as sequential perturbations designed to test the signaling network's resilience. Experiments would measure compensatory pathway activation and identify critical failure points in the cancer signaling network, potentially revealing optimal therapeutic sequences and combinations that exploit network vulnerabilities rather than just testing empirical drug combinations.

ideas.title: Prospectively map and pre-empt latent resistance networks to multi-node inhibition
ideas.description: This project shifts the paradigm from observing evolved resistance to predicting and neutralizing it before it emerges clinically. Using parallel, in vivo combinatorial genetic screens across diverse tumor models, it systematically identifies all potential genetic and non-genetic bypass pathways that could confer resistance to the multi-node therapy. These predicted vulnerabilities are then validated and ranked, informing the design of pre-emptive secondary therapies or rational triple-combination strategies. The approach is integrated with deep longitudinal ctDNA analysis in early-phase trials to detect the earliest signs of predicted resistance clones, enabling adaptive trial designs that switch or add therapies proactively to suppress outgrowth.

ideas.title: Cyclic Resource Modulation for Tumor Control
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of dynamic selective pressure from sustainable agriculture and integrated pest management to cancer therapy. In pest management, continuous high-dose pesticide use leads to resistant pest dominance; the analogous solution employs strategic rotations and habitat manipulations to suppress resistance evolution. Adapted to the oncology context, this involves applying ecological pressure models to manage tumor evolution under multi-node inhibition by designing treatment cycles that dynamically modulate which signaling pathways are targeted. The protocol would intentionally vary the 'landscape' of inhibition across different tumor signaling nodes (e.g., MAPK, PI3K, etc.) over time. This creates a heterogeneous and unpredictable selective environment within the tumor, disfavoring the evolution of clones resistant to a fixed combination therapy. The aim is to steer tumor evolution toward a more controllable, less aggressive state by exploiting ecological competition between sensitive and resistant cell populations, thereby extending the period of disease control.

ideas.title: Pan-cancer efficacy screen using a high-throughput, multi-parameter phenotyping platform
ideas.description: The original idea to scale up testing of a multi-node combination across a large cancer cell line panel is analogous to the problem in agronomy of screening thousands of plant cultivars for resilience to combined environmental stresses. That field solved it with high-throughput phenotyping platforms that apply controlled stress arrays and capture multi-parameter readouts (growth, physiology, health) in parallel. Adapted to cancer research, this involves implementing a scalable, automated screening platform that subjects a genomically-characterized, pan-cancer cell line panel to the multi-node combination. The platform would utilize multiplexed, high-content assays (e.g., imaging, flow cytometry) to simultaneously quantify a suite of phenotypic endpoints—viability, death mechanisms, morphological changes, and key signaling responses—across all lines. This generates a rich, multivariate efficacy dataset, linking combination sensitivity to cancer lineage and genetic features at scale, much like linking plant stress traits to genotypes.

ideas.title: Longitudinal CRISPR screening under adaptive therapy pressure to map synthetic lethality and escape pathways
ideas.description: This approach subjects a pooled CRISPR-knockout cell population to continuous multi-node inhibitor treatment in a serial passaging scheme, mimicking the evolutionary pressure of therapy. By tracking guide RNA depletion dynamics over time, it distinguishes between static synthetic lethal hits and those whose loss enables the emergence of resistant clones. This identifies the genetic vulnerabilities that are most robust to adaptive rewiring and, crucially, reveals the secondary pathways that cells co-opt to escape lethality. The output is a temporal map of targetable vulnerabilities and their associated resistance mechanisms, providing a rational sequence for combination or alternating therapy regimens to achieve durable suppression.

ideas.description: This research direction aims to develop a precision medicine framework that combines pharmacological agents for insulin suppression with a biomarker panel predictive of excessive pathway inhibition. The core objective is to identify and validate circulating or imaging biomarkers that signal the threshold between therapeutic insulin suppression and toxic over-inhibition of the pathway. By monitoring these biomarkers in real-time, the pharmacological intervention can be precisely calibrated to allow for significant relaxation of strict insulin-suppressing dietary regimens, thereby improving patient adherence and quality of life. This synergy moves beyond simply replacing diet with drugs to creating an adaptive, biomarker-informed dosing system that maximizes therapeutic benefit while preemptively avoiding toxicity.

ideas.description: This project moves beyond simple docking conservation to dissect whether shared pathway functions arise from the same mechanistic constraints. For a chosen signaling pathway, deep mutational scanning of a key protein in human and Drosophila cells identifies residues essential for function in each species. Essential residues are then tested for cross-species complementation by swapping orthologous domains and measuring functional rescue. This integrated approach definitively separates cases of direct mechanistic conservation from those where functional output is preserved through divergent molecular solutions.

ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to network stability analysis from electrical power systems engineering to the problem of quantifying metabolic rewiring in cancer. In power grids, engineers inject controlled perturbations and use sensor networks (PMUs) to trace disturbance propagation, mapping how power is rerouted around failed nodes. Adapted to cancer metabolism, we will use multi-node pharmacological inhibition as the controlled perturbation and employ dynamic isotopologue tracing as the high-resolution sensor network. This approach will move beyond static flux quantification to dynamically trace how the metabolic disturbance propagates, systematically identifying and ranking the compensatory pathways and emergent vulnerabilities that arise when primary metabolic nodes are inhibited.

ideas.title: Adapting Network Failure Prediction from Power Grids to Identify Allosteric Hotspots
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from power grid engineering to the problem of predicting allosteric hotspots. In power grids, methods to predict cascading failures analyze historical performance data to identify critical, interdependent nodes whose disruption destabilizes the entire network. We adapt this solution by treating a protein as a dynamical network, where residues are nodes and co-evolutionary couplings inferred from sequence alignments via Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) are the links. The adapted approach uses statistical network metrics inspired by failure prediction (like functional centrality and coupling strength) to identify residues with strong, distributed co-evolutionary connections to the active site. These residues are predicted to be allosteric hotspots, as their perturbation is most likely to propagate conformational change through the protein's structure, analogous to a critical node causing a grid-wide failure.

ideas.title: Closed-loop predictive system for individualized inhibitory load management
ideas.description: This system moves beyond static toxicity biomarkers to a dynamic, predictive framework for managing inhibitory therapies. It first establishes a patient-specific model of their expected therapeutic-inhibitory window using baseline multi-omic, neuroimaging, and clinical data. During treatment, a closed-loop monitoring array tracks real-time physiological and molecular signals of inhibitory load, comparing them against the personalized model. This allows for pre-emptive, algorithm-guided dose adjustments to maintain efficacy while staying safely within the individual's predicted tolerance limits, preventing toxicity rather than merely detecting it.

ideas.title: Biomarkers of Excessive Inhibition: An Ecological Resilience Analogy
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from ecosystem ecology. The analogous problem is predicting the collapse of an ecosystem under stress, which is solved by identifying indicator species and statistical early-warning signals (EWS) of declining resilience. The adapted research idea applies this framework to pharmacological inhibition. The goal is to find biomarkers—both biological (e.g., specific metabolite ratios) and statistical (e.g., changes in variance or autocorrelation within physiological time-series data)—that act as EWS for a patient's system approaching a toxic 'regime shift' due to excessive drug-induced inhibition. This would provide a dynamic, predictive tool for guiding safe dosing, moving beyond static biomarker thresholds to measures of systemic fragility.

ideas.title: Adapting Control Theory from Aerospace Engineering to Model Biological Feedback for Dosing
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from aerospace engineering control systems to the problem of optimal therapeutic dosing. The analogous field is Aerospace Engineering, specifically flight control systems, where control theory and state-space modeling are used to design autopilots that monitor aircraft state, predict dynamics, and apply real-time corrections to maintain stability. The adapted solution applies these control theory frameworks to biological pathway models. By treating a patient's molecular or physiological profile as a state vector and the drug dose as a control input, optimal control algorithms (e.g., Model Predictive Control) can be used to computationally solve for dosing regimens that steer the biological system toward a therapeutic target while explicitly optimizing for safety and efficacy constraints, moving beyond static models to dynamic, feedback-driven treatment scheduling.

ideas.title: Closed-loop, data-assimilating platform for adaptive dose personalization
ideas.description: This research system combines a mechanistic model of pathway dynamics with a real-time data assimilation engine to overcome the limitation of unknown patient-specific parameters. A Bayesian filtering framework continuously integrates sparse, noisy clinical data streams to infer the hidden state of the patient and the most likely model parameters, along with their uncertainty. The system then solves a stochastic optimal control problem to recommend a dosing regimen that is robust to this uncertainty. This creates a self-calibrating, adaptive platform where model predictions become increasingly personalized and reliable with each new clinical measurement, enabling truly precision dosing.

ideas.title: Pharmacological Insulin Suppression as a Targeted Replacement for Dietary Restriction
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that a specific, restrictive diet is a necessary component for achieving therapeutic insulin suppression. Drawing an analogy from psychiatry and addiction medicine, where pharmacological substitution therapy (e.g., methadone for opioid dependence) replaced the sole reliance on behavioral abstinence, we propose adapting this solution. The analogous problem of requiring strict behavioral change (abstinence/diet) for a physiological outcome (preventing withdrawal/maintaining low insulin) was solved by developing receptor-targeted agents that provide a controlled, sustained effect. Adapted to this context, the research would investigate if a pharmacological agent—such as a selective, tunable inhibitor of insulin secretion or a modulator of hepatic insulin sensitivity—can safely and effectively replace the dietary requirement for insulin suppression. This would decouple the metabolic goal from rigid dietary constraints, potentially improving adherence and outcomes in conditions like type 2 diabetes, obesity, or polycystic ovary syndrome where hyperinsulinemia is a key driver.
